</think>

Inflation refers to the sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services in an economy over time, resulting in a decrease in the purchasing power of money. Understanding this economic phenomenon requires looking at its drivers and subsequent impacts on financial stability and society.

Causes of Inflation
Several fundamental factors drive upward pressure on prices. **Excess demand** occurs when the aggregate demand for goods and services exceeds the current supply, leading to competition among buyers that drives up costs. **Cost-push factors** arise when production expenses, such as wages or raw material costs, rise, forcing businesses to pass these increased expenses onto consumers. Additionally, **expansionary monetary policy** implemented by central banks can lead to too much money chasing too few goods, stimulating price increases. Furthermore, **supply shocks**, such as natural disasters or geopolitical conflicts disrupting resource availability, can cause sudden scarcity and higher pricing. Lastly, **built-in inflation expectations** play a role where workers demand higher wages to match rising prices, creating a wage-price spiral that perpetuates the cycle.

Effects of Inflation
The repercussions of inflation extend beyond the grocery bill to affect broader economic health. **Reduced purchasing power** means that individuals require more currency to purchase the same amount of goods, eroding savings and living standards. **Currency devaluation** often occurs as foreign exchange reserves lose value, which can complicate international trade and imports. Central banks may respond by raising **interest rates**, which makes borrowing more expensive for consumers and businesses, potentially slowing investment and employment growth. Wealth distribution becomes uneven as creditors hold the real value of their loans while borrowers face repayment pressures, shifting assets from debtors to lenders. Long-term **uncertainty** can discourage business planning and innovation, as companies hesitate to commit to long-term projects due to unpredictable cost trajectories. Ultimately, unchecked inflation can lead to severe economic instability and reduced overall welfare.<|im_end|>

final_answer:
Inflation refers to the sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services in an economy over time, resulting in a decrease in the purchasing power of money. Understanding this economic phenomenon requires looking at its drivers and subsequent impacts on financial stability and society.

Causes of Inflation
Several fundamental factors drive upward pressure on prices. **Excess demand** occurs when the aggregate demand for goods and services exceeds the current supply, leading to competition among buyers that drives up costs. **Cost-push factors** arise when production expenses, such as wages or raw material costs, rise, forcing businesses to pass these increased expenses onto consumers. Additionally, **expansionary monetary policy** implemented by central banks can lead to too much money chasing too few goods, stimulating price increases. Furthermore, **supply shocks**, such as natural disasters or geopolitical conflicts disrupting resource availability, can cause sudden scarcity and higher pricing. Lastly, **built-in inflation expectations** play a role where workers demand higher wages to match rising prices, creating a wage-price spiral that perpetuates the cycle.

Effects of Inflation
The repercussions of inflation extend beyond the grocery bill to affect broader economic health. **Reduced purchasing power** means that individuals require more currency to purchase the same amount of goods, eroding savings and living standards. **Currency devaluation** often occurs as foreign exchange reserves lose value, which can complicate international trade and imports. Central banks may respond by raising **interest rates**, which makes borrowing more expensive for consumers and businesses, potentially slowing investment and employment growth. Wealth distribution becomes uneven as creditors hold the real value of their loans while borrowers face repayment pressures, shifting assets from debtors to lenders. Long-term **uncertainty** can discourage business planning and innovation, as companies hesitate to commit to long-term projects due to unpredictable cost trajectories. Ultimately, unchecked inflation can lead to severe economic instability and reduced overall welfare.<|im_end|>
